{{ill|Q135022904|lt=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=山那神社 | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
拝殿 | 所在地={{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|丹羽郡|Q1347719}}{{jalink|扶桑町|Q1203107}}大字南山名字森58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=山那神社}} | 祭神={{jalink|八野若比売命}} {{jalink|日本武尊}} {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫命}} {{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}} {{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}} {{jalink|仲哀天皇|Q179971}} | 社格=旧{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}、{{jalink|式内社}}論社 | 創建=不明 | 本殿={{jalink|神明造|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=山那神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''山那神社'''（やなじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|丹羽郡|Q1347719}}{{jalink|扶桑町|Q1203107}}{{jalink|南山名|Q97747108}}にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。 === 概略 === 創建時期は不明。しかし{{jalink|正暦|Q1324858}}年間に社殿を修復したという棟札があることから、相当古いことがわかる。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}は天神社と称していた。{{jalink|文化 (元号)|Q1373146|文化年間}}に{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}の裁定により南山名の天神社が式内社山那神社とされた。 {{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年）に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}における郷社となり、{{jalink|1902年|Q2043}}（明治35年）3月に山那神社に改称する。 {{jalink|1910年|Q2075}}（明治43年）に、高塚と本郷にあった八幡社二社を合祀した。さらに{{jalink|1920年|Q2155}}（{{jalink|大正|Q329174}}9年）には大名神社を合祀した。 === 由緒 === 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|式内社}}』に丹羽郡22座のうち「山那神社」とあり、『尾張志』には「南山名に坐て天神社と称す。延喜神名式に丹羽ノ郡山那ノ神社、『本国帳』に同郡従三位山名天神とある是なり。」とある。 『尾張地名考』には「山名村南北にわかれしのち、社は南山名につけり」とあり、『尾張神名帳集説乃訂考』に「山那ノ神社ニ社あり、文化年間八幡（南）の社人沢木氏と天神（北）社人尾関氏と争いしが、沢木氏は世ヶの社務職なるにより御裁許に勝ちし」という。また同書は『寛文村ヶ覚書』にある南山名の大明神、八幡、天神の三社のうち、「大館高門曰、南山名村八幡宮是なるべし」と記されている。 扶桑町には、南山名と{{jalink|山那|Q106705806}}の2箇所に同名の山那神社がある。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}はどちらも天神社を名乗っていたが、それぞれ式内社山那神社であることを主張していた。{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}の裁定により南山名の天神社が式内社山那神社とされた。この地域は{{jalink|木曽川|Q779607}}の洪水多発地帯であり、かつての山那神社も被害を受けていたことや、{{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}に戦乱で荒廃したことなどにより、所在がはっきりしていなかったためという。別説では、{{jalink|山名村|Q21655059}}が北山名村と南山名村に分離したさい、山那神社を2つに分祀したともいう。 === 祭神 === * {{jalink|八野若比売命}} ** 八野若比売命は{{jalink|出雲国風土記|Q11395858}}に登場する女神で、{{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|須佐能袁命}}の娘で{{jalink|大国主|Q276944|大穴持命}}の妃。 * {{jalink|日本武尊}} === 合祀 === * 八幡社（高塚八幡社） - 本郷81番地にあった。1910年（明治43年）合祀。一説によると多和田八幡といい、1301年（正安3年）4月、多和田七右エ衛門尉為氏の勧請と伝えられる。 ** {{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}} {{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}} {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫命}} * 八幡社（本郷八幡社） - 高塚22番地にあった。1910年（明治43年）合祀。 ** {{jalink|仲哀天皇|Q179971}} * 大明神社 - 神地にあった。1920年（大正9年）合祀。 ** {{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|素盞嗚命}} === ケヤキ === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|山那神社のケヤキ]] 境内には神社の古い歴史を物語るよう「{{jalink|ケヤキ|Q920344}}」の巨木があり、扶桑町指定文化財となっている。樹高30m、根回り4.9m、幹周4m、東西枝張8～9m6本、南北枝張5～6m6本。このケヤキは相当古く、文化的価値があるとされ、{{jalink|天然記念物|Q23790}}に指定された。 === 舞台 === {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}から{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}では{{jalink|舞台|Q194428}}が盛んで、各地神社の「籠堂」で芝居をよく上演した。{{jalink|南山名村|Q132044475}}では{{jalink|廻り舞台|Q253227}}や{{jalink|化粧室}}を備えた「舞台」まで造って熱狂したという。これには同村本郷の人で、中村七賀十郎を名乗り、東海一円を巡業するとともに地域の若者たちに熱心な{{jalink|歌舞伎|Q199701}}指導をした座長役者・{{jalink|小室仲太郎|Q124483423}}の存在が大きい。彼の没後、{{jalink|芝居|Q15096475}}は下火になり、舞台は次第に青年団の武道訓練場となり、1939年、地域の芸達者による芝居を最後に{{jalink|大日本帝国陸軍|Q276535|陸軍}}の物資倉庫に徴用された。終戦後は{{jalink|復員兵}}の歓迎演芸会や役者を呼んでの買い芝居も行われたが、昔日の舞台復活とはならなかった。建物は地区の集会場や農産物集荷場として利用されることが多くなり、廻り舞台は土砂で埋められ、{{jalink|公民館|Q8194637}}として利用しやすく玄関を作り、化粧室をなくして小型にされた。その後、機能的な公民館、集会所が他所にできて、建物は再び倉庫に戻って生き延びたが、老朽化してきた。文化遺産「舞台」としての保存も陳情したが、改修によって舞台とは言えない形となってしまったため、認定は無理だった。この結果から何度も議論して、取り壊すこととなった。2013年（平成25年）10月、舞台の記念碑が建立された。 === 所在地 === * 愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58 ** {{jalink|扶桑町立山名小学校|Q81976214}}の南、約500m。 === 交通機関 === * {{jalink|名古屋鉄道|Q30850|名鉄}}{{jalink|名鉄犬山線|Q6810411|犬山線}} {{jalink|扶桑駅|Q5510430}}より徒歩で約20分。 === 関連項目 === * {{jalink|尾張国の式内社一覧|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|山那神社 (扶桑町山那)|Q119929641}} === 参考文献 === * 『扶桑町史』、{{jalink|扶桑町|Q1203107}}、{{jalink|1998年|Q2089}}、第二章宗教、505頁。 === 脚注 === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=山那神社 | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
拝殿 | 所在地={{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|丹羽郡|Q1347719}}{{jalink|扶桑町|Q1203107}}大字南山名字森58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=山那神社}} | 祭神={{jalink|八野若比売命}} {{jalink|日本武尊}} {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫命}} {{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}} {{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}} {{jalink|仲哀天皇|Q179971}} | 社格=旧{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}、{{jalink|式内社}}論社 | 創建=不明 | 本殿={{jalink|神明造|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=山那神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''山那神社'''（やなじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|愛知県|Q80434}}{{jalink|丹羽郡|Q1347719}}{{jalink|扶桑町|Q1203107}}{{jalink|南山名|Q97747108}}にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。 === 概略 === 創建時期は不明。しかし{{jalink|正暦|Q1324858}}年間に社殿を修復したという棟札があることから、相当古いことがわかる。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}は天神社と称していた。{{jalink|文化 (元号)|Q1373146|文化年間}}に{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}の裁定により南山名の天神社が式内社山那神社とされた。 {{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}（{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年）に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}における郷社となり、{{jalink|1902年|Q2043}}（明治35年）3月に山那神社に改称する。 {{jalink|1910年|Q2075}}（明治43年）に、高塚と本郷にあった八幡社二社を合祀した。さらに{{jalink|1920年|Q2155}}（{{jalink|大正|Q329174}}9年）には大名神社を合祀した。 === 由緒 === 『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|式内社}}』に丹羽郡22座のうち「山那神社」とあり、『尾張志』には「南山名に坐て天神社と称す。延喜神名式に丹羽ノ郡山那ノ神社、『本国帳』に同郡従三位山名天神とある是なり。」とある。 『尾張地名考』には「山名村南北にわかれしのち、社は南山名につけり」とあり、『尾張神名帳集説乃訂考』に「山那ノ神社ニ社あり、文化年間八幡（南）の社人沢木氏と天神（北）社人尾関氏と争いしが、沢木氏は世ヶの社務職なるにより御裁許に勝ちし」という。また同書は『寛文村ヶ覚書』にある南山名の大明神、八幡、天神の三社のうち、「大館高門曰、南山名村八幡宮是なるべし」と記されている。 扶桑町には、南山名と{{jalink|山那|Q106705806}}の2箇所に同名の山那神社がある。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}はどちらも天神社を名乗っていたが、それぞれ式内社山那神社であることを主張していた。{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}の裁定により南山名の天神社が式内社山那神社とされた。この地域は{{jalink|木曽川|Q779607}}の洪水多発地帯であり、かつての山那神社も被害を受けていたことや、{{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}に戦乱で荒廃したことなどにより、所在がはっきりしていなかったためという。別説では、{{jalink|山名村|Q21655059}}が北山名村と南山名村に分離したさい、山那神社を2つに分祀したともいう。 === 祭神 === * {{jalink|八野若比売命}} ** 八野若比売命は{{jalink|出雲国風土記|Q11395858}}に登場する女神で、{{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|須佐能袁命}}の娘で{{jalink|大国主|Q276944|大穴持命}}の妃。 * {{jalink|日本武尊}} === 合祀 === * 八幡社（高塚八幡社） - 本郷81番地にあった。1910年（明治43年）合祀。一説によると多和田八幡といい、1301年（正安3年）4月、多和田七右エ衛門尉為氏の勧請と伝えられる。 ** {{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}} {{jalink|応神天皇|Q317997}} {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫命}} * 八幡社（本郷八幡社） - 高塚22番地にあった。1910年（明治43年）合祀。 ** {{jalink|仲哀天皇|Q179971}} * 大明神社 - 神地にあった。1920年（大正9年）合祀。 ** {{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|素盞嗚命}} === ケヤキ === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|山那神社のケヤキ]] 境内には神社の古い歴史を物語るよう「{{jalink|ケヤキ|Q920344}}」の巨木があり、扶桑町指定文化財となっている。樹高30m、根回り4.9m、幹周4m、東西枝張8～9m6本、南北枝張5～6m6本。このケヤキは相当古く、文化的価値があるとされ、{{jalink|天然記念物|Q23790}}に指定された。 === 舞台 === {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}から{{jalink|尾張藩|Q1062072}}では{{jalink|舞台|Q194428}}が盛んで、各地神社の「籠堂」で芝居をよく上演した。{{jalink|南山名村|Q132044475}}では{{jalink|廻り舞台|Q253227}}や{{jalink|化粧室}}を備えた「舞台」まで造って熱狂したという。これには同村本郷の人で、中村七賀十郎を名乗り、東海一円を巡業するとともに地域の若者たちに熱心な{{jalink|歌舞伎|Q199701}}指導をした座長役者・{{jalink|小室仲太郎|Q124483423}}の存在が大きい。彼の没後、{{jalink|芝居|Q15096475}}は下火になり、舞台は次第に青年団の武道訓練場となり、1939年、地域の芸達者による芝居を最後に{{jalink|大日本帝国陸軍|Q276535|陸軍}}の物資倉庫に徴用された。終戦後は{{jalink|復員兵}}の歓迎演芸会や役者を呼んでの買い芝居も行われたが、昔日の舞台復活とはならなかった。建物は地区の集会場や農産物集荷場として利用されることが多くなり、廻り舞台は土砂で埋められ、{{jalink|公民館|Q8194637}}として利用しやすく玄関を作り、化粧室をなくして小型にされた。その後、機能的な公民館、集会所が他所にできて、建物は再び倉庫に戻って生き延びたが、老朽化してきた。文化遺産「舞台」としての保存も陳情したが、改修によって舞台とは言えない形となってしまったため、認定は無理だった。この結果から何度も議論して、取り壊すこととなった。2013年（平成25年）10月、舞台の記念碑が建立された。 === 所在地 === * 愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58 ** {{jalink|扶桑町立山名小学校|Q81976214}}の南、約500m。 === 交通機関 === * {{jalink|名古屋鉄道|Q30850|名鉄}}{{jalink|名鉄犬山線|Q6810411|犬山線}} {{jalink|扶桑駅|Q5510430}}より徒歩で約20分。 === 関連項目 === * {{jalink|尾張国の式内社一覧|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|山那神社 (扶桑町山那)|Q119929641}} === 参考文献 === * 『扶桑町史』、{{jalink|扶桑町|Q1203107}}、{{jalink|1998年|Q2089}}、第二章宗教、505頁。 === 脚注 === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}

{{ill|Q135022904|lt=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}

{{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}

{{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}

{{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}

{{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}[[Category:qq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}{{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}{{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} | 社格=former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}, {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{jalink|shinmei-zukuri|Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Minamiyana|Q97747108}}, {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|Niwa District|Q1347719}}, {{jalink|Aichi Prefecture|Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1873|Q7757}}（{{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}; in March {{jalink|1902|Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{jalink|1910|Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{jalink|1920|Q2155}}（{{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{jalink|Yana|Q106705806}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{jalink|Kiso River|Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{jalink|Sengoku period|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{jalink|Yana village|Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{jalink|Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858}}, daughter of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{jalink|Yamato Takeru}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} {{jalink|Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{jalink|Emperor Chūai|Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the {{jalink|Owari Domain|Q1062072}} had flourishing {{jalink|stages|Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{jalink|Minamiyana Village|Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{jalink|revolving stage|Q253227}} and {{jalink|dressing room}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{jalink|Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{jalink|kabuki|Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{jalink|Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{jalink|community center|Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{jalink|Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Fusō Station|Q5510430}} on the {{jalink|Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|Inuyama Line}} of {{jalink|Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{jalink|List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292}} * {{jalink|Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{jalink|Fusō|Q1203107}}, {{jalink|1998|Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{ill|Q135022904|lt=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill||lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} {{ill||lt=Yamato Takeru|UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}, {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill||lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto}}. * {{ill||lt=Yamato Takeru|UNKNOWN}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227}} and {{ill||lt=dressing room|UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
