== name in kana (P1814) == * やなじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9180005009275 == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Yamanano Shrine|lt=Yamanano Shrine|WD=Q135039500|1=Yamanano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やなしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QYana Shrine (Q11471014)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Yana Shrine (Q11471014)}}[[Category:qq]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * やなじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9180005009275 == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Yamanano Shrine|lt=Yamanano Shrine|WD=Q135039500|1=Yamanano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]] [[Category:qq]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社|論]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * やなじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9180005009275 == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Yamanano Shrine|lt=Yamanano Shrine|WD=Q135039500|1=Yamanano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:愛知県の旧郷社]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * やなじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9180005009275 == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Yamanano Shrine|lt=Yamanano Shrine|WD=Q135039500|1=Yamanano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Aichi Prefecture]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * やなじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9180005009275 == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Yamanano Shrine|lt=Yamanano Shrine|WD=Q135039500|1=Yamanano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Aichi Prefecture]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * やなじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 9180005009275 == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Yamanano Shrine|lt=Yamanano Shrine|WD=Q135039500|1=Yamanano Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町大字南山名字森58', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '愛知県丹羽郡扶桑町山那', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:山那神社 (扶桑町南山名)]] {{wikidata link|Q11471014}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Yana Shrine | 画像 = [[File:Yanajinja(Minamiyana).jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}{{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 | 位置={{coord|35|22|3.2|N|136|54|23|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yana Shrine}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} | 社格=former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine | 創建=Unknown | 本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | 例祭= | 神事= | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Yana Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yana Shrine'''（やなじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamiyana|Q97747108|WD=Q97747108|1=Minamiyana, Fuso}}, {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Niwa District|Q1347719|WD=Q1347719|1=Niwa District}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. === Overview === The date of foundation is unknown. However, from a ridge tag stating that the shrine building was repaired during the {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858|1=Shōryaku}} era, it is known to be very old. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it was called Tenjin-sha. In the {{ill|lt=Bunka era|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka years|1=Bunka}}, by judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, the Tenjin-sha of Minamiyana was identified as the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}（{{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6）, it became a gōsha under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}; in March {{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043|1=1902}}（Meiji 35）, it was renamed Yana Shrine. In {{ill|lt=1910|Q2075|WD=Q2075|1=1910}}（Meiji 43）, two Hachiman shrines in Takatsuka and Hongō were merged into it. In {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|WD=Q2155|1=1920}}（{{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 9）, Daimyōjin Shrine was also merged. === Origins === In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha list|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'', among 22 seats of Niwa District appears "Yana Shrine." In ''Owari-shi'' it says, "Located at Minamiyana and called Tenjin-sha. In the Engishiki Jinmyō list it is Yana Shrine of Niwa District; in Honkoku-chō it appears as Minamiyana Tenjin of Junior Third Rank in the same district." ''Owari Chimeikō'' says, "After Yana village was divided into south and north, the shrine was attached to Minamiyana." ''Owari Shinmeichō Shūsetsu Nara Teikō'' says, "There were two Yana shrines; in the Bunka era, the shrine family Sawaki of Hachiman (south) and shrine family Ozeki of Tenjin (north) contested, and Sawaki won by virtue of hereditary shrine-office service." The same work, citing ''Kanbun Sonson Oboegaki,'' records that among the three Minamiyana shrines (Daimyōjin, Hachiman, Tenjin), "Ōdate Takakado states that this should be Hachimangū of Minamiyana village." In Fusō there are two shrines with the same name, Yana Shrine, in Minamiyana and {{ill|lt=Yana|Q106705806|WD=Q106705806|1=Yamana, Fuso}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, both used the name Tenjin-sha and each claimed to be the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. By judgment of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}, Minamiyana's Tenjin-sha was designated the shikinaisha Yana Shrine. This area was prone to floods of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and because the former Yana Shrine suffered damage and also fell into ruin during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, its location became unclear. Another theory says that when {{ill|lt=Yana village|Q21655059|WD=Q21655059|1=Yamana}} split into north and south villages, Yana Shrine was divided into two. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Yano-wakabime-no-Mikoto is a goddess appearing in the {{ill|lt=Izumo Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}, daughter of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanō-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} and consort of {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} === Merged shrines === * Hachiman Shrine（Takatsuka Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Hongō lot 81; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. One theory says it was called Tawada Hachiman and was founded by enshrinement from Tawada Shichiuemon Tameuji in April 1301（Shōan 3）. ** {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyūga myth)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} * Hachiman Shrine（Hongō Hachiman Shrine）- formerly at Takatsuka lot 22; merged in 1910（Meiji 43）. ** {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} * Daimyōjin Shrine - formerly in shrine grounds; merged in 1920（Taishō 9）. ** {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} === Zelkova === [[File:山那神社のケヤキ.png|thumb|Zelkova tree of Yana Shrine]] In the precincts stands a giant {{ill|lt=zelkova|Q920344|WD=Q920344|1=Zelkova serrata}} that seems to tell the shrine's long history; it is designated a Fusō Town cultural property. Height 30 m, root circumference 4.9 m, trunk circumference 4 m, six east-west branches of 8-9 m, and six north-south branches of 5-6 m. This tree is very old and culturally valuable, and has been designated a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Stage === From the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}} had flourishing {{ill|lt=stages|Q194428|WD=Q194428|1=Stage (theatre)}}, and plays were often performed in shrine "kaguradō" halls. In {{ill|lt=Minamiyana Village|Q132044475|WD=Q132044475|1=Minamiyana Village}}, they became so enthusiastic that they built a full "stage" equipped with a {{ill|lt=revolving stage|Q253227|WD=Q253227|1=Revolving stage}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dressing room|1=UNKNOWN}}. A major reason was the presence of actor-manager {{ill|lt=Komuro Chūtarō|Q124483423|WD=Q124483423|1=Komuro Nakatarō}}, a native of Hongō in the same village, who toured across Tōkai and passionately trained local youth in {{ill|lt=kabuki|Q199701|WD=Q199701|1=Kabuki}}. After his death, performances declined, the stage gradually became a martial-arts training hall for youth groups, and in 1939, after a final local performance, it was requisitioned as a supply warehouse by the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|WD=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}. After the war, welcome entertainment for demobilized soldiers and bought-in plays were held, but the old stage culture did not revive. The building came to be used mainly as a meeting hall and farm-produce collection site; the revolving stage was buried, an entrance was built for easier use as a {{ill|lt=community center|Q8194637|WD=Q8194637|1=Kominkan}}, and the dressing room was removed, making it smaller. Later, with functional community halls built elsewhere, the building survived again as a warehouse but became deteriorated. Preservation was petitioned as cultural heritage "stage," but since renovation had left it no longer recognizable as a true stage, certification was deemed impossible. After repeated discussion, it was demolished. In October 2013（Heisei 25）, a commemorative monument to the stage was erected. === Address === * Aichi Prefecture, Niwa District, Fusō, Ōaza Minamiyana, aza Mori 58 ** About 500 m south of {{ill|lt=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School|Q81976214|WD=Q81976214|1=Fusō Municipal Yana Elementary School}}. === Transportation === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Fusō Station|Q5510430|WD=Q5510430|1=Fusō Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Inuyama Line|Q6810411|WD=Q6810411|lt=Inuyama Line|1=Meitetsu Inuyama Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of shikinaisha in Owari Province|Q11465292|WD=Q11465292|1=List of Shikinaisha in Owari Province}} * {{ill|lt=Yana Shrine (Fusō Yana)|Q119929641|WD=Q119929641|1=Yana Shrine}} === References === * ''History of Fusō Town'', {{ill|lt=Fusō|Q1203107|WD=Q1203107|1=Fusō, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}}, Chapter 2 Religion, p. 505. === Notes === {{Reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yana Shrine (Q11471014)]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:南山名]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:扶桑町の歴史]] [[Category:扶桑町の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Aichi Prefecture]]
